text: The laparoscopic view reveals a frozen pelvis with adhesions involving bowel loops, obscuring the visualization of the uterus, bilateral fallopian tubes, and ovaries.
The surgeon begins adhesiolysis, applying traction and counter-traction techniques to carefully separate adhesions within the pelvic cavity.
During the application of traction, a white line becomes visible between the two structures, indicating a clear plane for dissection.
During the adhesiolysis procedure for endometriosis, the surgeon carefully spares the peritoneum while separating adhesions.
The surgeon uses cold scissors to perform adhesiolysis, minimizing lateral thermal damage while carefully dissecting adhesions in the pelvic region.
The surgeon uses cold scissors to dissect in the correct planes, carefully avoiding adhesions and preventing blood loss while ensuring no injury to adjacent structures.

The uterus is visualized, and vasopressin is injected into the myelodendron using a syringe, with a dilution of twenty units in one hundred ml or forty units in two hundred ml. This injection aids in adhesiolysis and reduces blood loss.
